questions.text: Azolla is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Aquatic Fern**

Azolla is a small floating **aquatic fern** commonly used as a biofertilizer in paddy fields.

Option Analysis:
• Aquatic Fern → Correct. Azolla belongs to pteridophytes.
• Aquatic algae → Incorrect. Although it grows with algae (Anabaena), it itself is a fern.
• Aquatic bacteria → Incorrect.
• None of the above → Incorrect.

Exam Insight:
Azolla lives in symbiosis with **Anabaena azollae**, which fixes atmospheric nitrogen.

questions.text: Aquatic Fern, which is used as a biofertilizer is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Azolla**

Azolla is widely used in rice cultivation as a biofertilizer.

Option Analysis:
• Salvinia → Aquatic fern but not widely used as biofertilizer.
• Azolla → Correct. Contains nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria.
• Marsilia → Aquatic fern but not used as fertilizer.
• Pteridium → Terrestrial fern.

questions.text: Which of the following microorganisms are used as biofertilizer?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Cyanobacteria**

Cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) fix atmospheric nitrogen and enrich soil fertility.

Option Analysis:
• Cyanobacteria → Correct. Examples include Nostoc and Anabaena.
• Protozoa → Mostly heterotrophic and not used as fertilizers.
• Virus → Infective agents; no role in soil fertility.
• None of the above → Incorrect.

questions.text: The capability of nitrogen use efficiency can be increased in crop production by
options: Using the slow release of nitrogen fertilizer
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: All of the above**

Nitrogen use efficiency increases when nitrogen is supplied gradually or controlled.

Option Analysis:
• Split application → Prevents nitrogen loss.
• Nitrogen inhibitors → Reduce nitrification losses.
• Slow-release fertilizers → Provide nitrogen steadily.
• All of the above → Correct.

questions.text:
Which of the following is/are used as biofertilizers?
1. Azolla
2. Blue-green algae
3. Alfalfa
Select the correct answer by the given codes.
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: 1 and 2**

Azolla and blue-green algae are important biological nitrogen fixers used as biofertilizers.

questions.text: How many chromosomes are there in humans?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: 46**

Human somatic cells contain **46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs**.

questions.text: The number of chromosomes pairs found in the cells of human body is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: 23**

Humans have **23 pairs of chromosomes**.

questions.text: Which organelle in the cell, other than the nucleus, contains DNA?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Mitochondrion**

Mitochondria contain their own circular DNA called **mtDNA**.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct?
options: Prions are the smallest free-living cells
The cell wall of Mycoplasmas is made up of amino sugars
Viroids consist of single-stranded RNA molecules
questions.solution:
This question is from **microbiology and acellular organisms**, a topic that sometimes appears in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which one of the following statements is correct?**

A. Prions are the smallest free-living cells
B. The cell wall of Mycoplasmas is made up of amino sugars
C. Viroids consist of single-stranded RNA molecules
D. Rickettsias lack cell wall

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Viroids consist of single-stranded RNA molecules**

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Prions are the smallest free-living cells ❌ (Incorrect)

**Prion**

Prions are:

• **Protein particles**
• **Do not contain DNA or RNA**
• **Not living cells**

They cause diseases such as:

• Mad cow disease
• Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease

Thus they are **not free-living cells**.

---

## B. The cell wall of Mycoplasmas is made up of amino sugars ❌ (Incorrect)

**Mycoplasma**

Mycoplasma actually **does not have a cell wall at all**.

Instead it has a **sterol-containing plasma membrane**.

Thus this statement is incorrect.

---

## C. Viroids consist of single-stranded RNA molecules ✔ (Correct)

**Viroid**

Viroids:

• Are **smaller than viruses**
• Consist only of **circular single-stranded RNA**
• **Do not have protein coats**

They infect **plants** and cause diseases such as **potato spindle tuber disease**.

Thus this statement is correct.

---

## D. Rickettsias lack cell wall ❌ (Incorrect)

**Rickettsia**

Rickettsia are:

• **Gram-negative bacteria**
• Have a **cell wall**

Thus the statement that they lack a cell wall is incorrect.

---

# Quick Summary

| Organism | Key Feature |
|---|---|
| Prion | Infectious protein |
| Mycoplasma | Smallest bacterium without cell wall |
| Viroid | Single-stranded RNA pathogen |
| Rickettsia | Intracellular bacteria with cell wall |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember these associations:

• **Prion → Protein only**
• **Viroid → RNA only**
• **Mycoplasma → No cell wall**

Thus the correct statement is:

**Viroids consist of single-stranded RNA molecules.**

questions.text: The reason of large-scale diversity among the organisms is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Mutation**

Mutations create genetic variations which drive evolution.

questions.text: Which of the following does not have sex-chromosome?
questions.solution:
This question is from **genetics and sex determination**, a topic that sometimes appears in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which of the following does not have sex chromosomes?**

A. Monkey
B. Tiger
C. Butterfly
D. Lizard

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Lizard**

---

# Core Concept

In many organisms, sex is determined by **sex chromosomes** such as:

• **X and Y chromosomes** in mammals
• **Z and W chromosomes** in birds and butterflies

However, some animals determine sex by **environmental factors**, such as **temperature**, and therefore **do not rely on sex chromosomes**.

This occurs in many **reptiles** like some lizards and turtles.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Monkey ❌ (Incorrect)

**Monkey**

Monkeys are **mammals**.

Mammals use **XY sex determination**:

• Male → XY
• Female → XX

Thus monkeys **have sex chromosomes**.

---

## B. Tiger ❌ (Incorrect)

**Tiger**

Tigers are also **mammals**.

They follow the **XY chromosome system**, so they **have sex chromosomes**.

---

## C. Butterfly ❌ (Incorrect)

**Butterfly**

Butterflies use the **ZW sex determination system**:

• Male → ZZ
• Female → ZW

Thus butterflies **do have sex chromosomes**.

---

## D. Lizard ✔ (Correct)

**Lizard**

Many reptiles (including several lizard species) determine sex by **temperature during egg development**.

This is called **Temperature-Dependent Sex Determination (TSD)**.

Since sex is determined by temperature rather than chromosomes, they **may not have sex chromosomes**.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Animal | Sex Determination |
|---|---|
| Monkey | XY chromosomes |
| Tiger | XY chromosomes |
| Butterfly | ZW chromosomes |
| Lizard | Temperature-dependent |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this pattern:

• **Mammals → XY system**
• **Birds / butterflies → ZW system**
• **Some reptiles → Temperature-based**

Thus the organism **without sex chromosomes** here is **Lizard**.

questions.text: How does the plant cell differ from the animal cell?
questions.solution:
This question is from **cell biology**, which is a common topic in Biology asked in **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**How does the plant cell differ from the animal cell?**

A. Mitochondria
B. Cell wall
C. Presence of protoplasm
D. Cell nucleus

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Cell wall**

---

# Core Concept

Plant cells and animal cells share many structures such as:

• **Nucleus**
• **Mitochondria**
• **Cytoplasm (protoplasm)**

However, **plant cells have a cell wall**, while **animal cells do not**.

The cell wall provides:

• **Strength**
• **Protection**
• **Shape to plant cells**

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Mitochondria ❌ (Incorrect)

**Mitochondrion**

Mitochondria are present in **both plant cells and animal cells**.

Function:

• Produce **ATP (energy)** through cellular respiration.

Thus mitochondria **do not differentiate plant cells from animal cells**.

---

## B. Cell wall ✔ (Correct)

**Cell wall**

Plant cells have a **cell wall made mainly of cellulose**.

Animal cells **lack a cell wall**.

This is a **major structural difference** between plant and animal cells.

---

## C. Presence of protoplasm ❌ (Incorrect)

**Protoplasm** refers to the **living content of the cell**, including:

• Cytoplasm
• Nucleus

Both plant and animal cells **contain protoplasm**.

Thus it is not a distinguishing feature.

---

## D. Cell nucleus ❌ (Incorrect)

The **Cell nucleus** is present in **both plant and animal cells**.

It controls:

• Cell activities
• Genetic information

Thus it does not differentiate the two.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Feature | Plant Cell | Animal Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Cell wall | Present | Absent |
| Chloroplast | Present | Absent |
| Mitochondria | Present | Present |
| Nucleus | Present | Present |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember the **three key features unique to plant cells**:

• **Cell wall**
• **Chloroplast**
• **Large central vacuole**

Thus the correct answer is **Cell wall**.

questions.text: What is not found in the animal cell?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Cell wall of cellulose**

Animal cells lack a rigid cell wall.

questions.text: One term that generates the maximum number of ATP atoms in our system?
questions.solution:
This question is from **cellular respiration and ATP production**, a topic sometimes asked in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which step generates the maximum number of ATP molecules in our body?**

A. Decomposition of glucose
B. Krebs cycle
C. Last respiratory series
D. Water decomposition

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Last respiratory series**

(The “last respiratory series” refers to the **electron transport chain**.)

---

# Core Concept

During **cellular respiration**, glucose is broken down to produce energy in the form of **ATP (adenosine triphosphate)**.

Respiration occurs in **three main stages**:

1. **Glycolysis** (glucose breakdown)
2. **Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)**
3. **Electron Transport Chain (ETS)** – final respiratory series

Most ATP is produced in the **electron transport chain**.

---

# ATP Production in Each Stage

| Stage | ATP Produced |
|---|---|
| Glycolysis | 2 ATP |
| Krebs cycle | 2 ATP |
| Electron transport chain | ~32–34 ATP |

Thus the **maximum ATP is produced in the electron transport chain**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Decomposition of glucose ❌ (Incorrect)

This refers to **glycolysis**, the first step of respiration.

Process:

• Glucose → Pyruvate

ATP produced:

• Only **2 ATP**

Thus it does not generate the maximum ATP.

---

## B. Krebs cycle ❌ (Incorrect)

The **Krebs cycle** occurs in the mitochondria.

Products:

• NADH
• FADH₂
• Small amount of ATP

ATP produced:

• **2 ATP**

Thus it is not the largest source of ATP.

---

## C. Last respiratory series ✔ (Correct)

The **Electron transport chain** occurs in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

Here:

• Electrons pass through a chain of proteins
• Energy released drives **ATP synthesis**

ATP produced:

• **~32–34 ATP**

Thus it generates the **maximum ATP**.

---

## D. Water decomposition ❌ (Incorrect)

Water decomposition (photolysis) occurs in **photosynthesis**, not cellular respiration.

Thus it is unrelated to ATP production in human cells.

---

# Quick Summary

| Stage of Respiration | ATP Yield |
|---|---|
| Glycolysis | 2 ATP |
| Krebs cycle | 2 ATP |
| Electron transport chain | Maximum ATP |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember the sequence:

**Glycolysis → Krebs cycle → Electron transport chain**

➡ **Maximum ATP is produced in the Electron Transport Chain (Last respiratory series)**.

questions.text: Which of the following organelle is called ‘Power House’ of the cell?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Mitochondria**

Mitochondria produce ATP via oxidative phosphorylation.

questions.text: Corpus luteum is a mass of cells found in
questions.solution:
This question is from **human reproductive biology**, a topic sometimes asked in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Corpus luteum is a mass of cells found in**

A. Brain
B. Ovary
C. Pancreas
D. Spleen

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Ovary**

---

# Core Concept

**Corpus luteum** is a **yellow-colored mass of cells** that forms in the **ovary after ovulation**.

Process:

1. An ovary releases an egg (ovulation).
2. The empty follicle transforms into the **corpus luteum**.
3. It secretes hormones such as **progesterone**.

Function:

• Prepares the uterus for **pregnancy**.
• Maintains the **uterine lining**.

If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum **degenerates into corpus albicans**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Brain ❌ (Incorrect)

The **brain** controls hormonal activities through glands like:

• **Pituitary gland**

However, the corpus luteum **is not found in the brain**.

---

## B. Ovary ✔ (Correct)

The **Ovary** contains follicles that develop eggs.

After ovulation:

• The follicle becomes the **corpus luteum**.

Thus the corpus luteum is located in the **ovary**.

---

## C. Pancreas ❌ (Incorrect)

The **Pancreas** regulates blood sugar.

Functions:

• Produces **insulin and glucagon**

It has **no role in corpus luteum formation**.

---

## D. Spleen ❌ (Incorrect)

The **Spleen** helps:

• Filter blood
• Support immune function

It is unrelated to reproduction.

---

# Quick Summary

| Structure | Location |
|---|---|
| Corpus luteum | Ovary |
| Pituitary gland | Brain |
| Pancreas | Digestive & endocrine system |
| Spleen | Immune system |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this sequence in the **female reproductive cycle**:

**Follicle → Ovulation → Corpus luteum**

So the corpus luteum is found in the **ovary**.

questions.text: Plasma membrane is built up of
questions.solution:
This question is from **cell biology (structure of the plasma membrane)**, a topic commonly asked in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Plasma membrane is built up of**

A. Protein
B. Lipid
C. Carbohydrate
D. Both (A) and (B)

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Both (A) and (B)**

---

# Core Concept

The **Plasma membrane** forms the outer boundary of the cell.

According to the **Fluid Mosaic Model**, the plasma membrane mainly consists of:

• **Lipids (phospholipid bilayer)**
• **Proteins embedded in the membrane**

Structure:

[ Plasma membrane = Lipid bilayer + Proteins ]

Carbohydrates may also be present in small amounts, but the **main components are lipids and proteins**.

---

# Main Components of Plasma Membrane

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Lipids | Form the bilayer and provide flexibility |
| Proteins | Transport substances and act as receptors |
| Carbohydrates | Cell recognition and communication |

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Protein ❌ (Incomplete)

Proteins are an important part of the membrane, but **the membrane is not made of protein alone**.

Thus this option is incomplete.

---

## B. Lipid ❌ (Incomplete)

Lipids form the **phospholipid bilayer**, which is the structural base of the membrane.

However, proteins are also essential components.

Thus this option alone is not correct.

---

## C. Carbohydrate ❌ (Incorrect)

Carbohydrates occur in small amounts as:

• **Glycoproteins**
• **Glycolipids**

But they are **not the main structural components**.

---

## D. Both (A) and (B) ✔ (Correct)

The plasma membrane is mainly composed of:

• **Lipids**
• **Proteins**

Thus this option is correct.

---

# Quick Summary

| Component | Presence in Plasma Membrane |
|---|---|
| Lipids | Major component |
| Proteins | Major component |
| Carbohydrates | Minor component |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember the **Fluid Mosaic Model**:

**Membrane = Lipid bilayer + Proteins**

So the correct answer is **Both (A) and (B)**.

questions.text: Which of the following cell organelles play the most significant role in protein synthesis?
questions.solution:
This question is from **cell biology (protein synthesis and cell organelles)**, a topic commonly asked in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which of the following cell organelles play the most significant role in protein synthesis?**

A. Lysosome and Centrosome
B. Endoplasmic reticulum and Ribosome
C. Golgi apparatus and Mitochondria
D. Lysosome and Mitochondria

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Endoplasmic reticulum and Ribosome**

---

# Core Concept

**Protein synthesis** mainly occurs in **Ribosome**.

Ribosomes may be:

• Free in cytoplasm
• Attached to the **Rough endoplasmic reticulum**

Thus the organelles most directly involved in protein synthesis are:

• **Ribosomes**
• **Rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER)**

---

# How Protein Synthesis Occurs

1. DNA in nucleus forms **mRNA**.
2. mRNA moves to **ribosomes**.
3. Ribosomes assemble **amino acids into proteins**.
4. Rough ER helps in **processing and transporting proteins**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Lysosome and Centrosome ❌ (Incorrect)

**Lysosome**

• Breaks down waste materials.

**Centrosome**

• Helps form spindle fibers during mitosis.

Neither is involved in protein synthesis.

---

## B. Endoplasmic reticulum and Ribosome ✔ (Correct)

**Endoplasmic reticulum**

• Rough ER contains ribosomes.

**Ribosomes** synthesize proteins by linking amino acids.

Thus these two organelles are the **main sites of protein synthesis**.

---

## C. Golgi apparatus and Mitochondria ❌ (Incorrect)

**Golgi apparatus**

• Packages proteins after they are made.

**Mitochondrion**

• Produces energy.

Neither directly synthesizes proteins.

---

## D. Lysosome and Mitochondria ❌ (Incorrect)

• Lysosome → digestion
• Mitochondria → energy production

They are not responsible for protein synthesis.

---

# Quick Summary

| Organelle | Function |
|---|---|
| Ribosome | Protein synthesis |
| Rough ER | Protein processing and transport |
| Golgi apparatus | Protein packaging |
| Lysosome | Waste digestion |
| Mitochondria | Energy production |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

**Protein synthesis → Ribosomes**

And ribosomes are often attached to **Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum**.

Thus the correct answer is:

**Endoplasmic reticulum and Ribosome**..

questions.text: Lymphocytes cells are formed in which part of human body?
questions.solution:
This question is from **human physiology and the immune system**, a topic that appears in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Lymphocyte cells are formed in which part of the human body?**

A. Liver
B. Long bone
C. Pancreas
D. Spleen

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Long bone**

---

# Core Concept

**Lymphocyte** are a type of **white blood cell (WBC)** that play a key role in the immune system.

Types of lymphocytes:

• **B-cells**
• **T-cells**
• **Natural killer (NK) cells**

These cells originate from **stem cells in the bone marrow**.

Bone marrow is located inside **long bones** such as:

• Femur
• Humerus

Thus lymphocytes are formed in the **bone marrow of long bones**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Liver ❌ (Incorrect)

**Liver**

Functions:

• Detoxification
• Bile production
• Metabolism

The liver **does not produce lymphocytes in adults**.

---

## B. Long bone ✔ (Correct)

Inside long bones is **bone marrow**, where blood cells are produced.

Process:

[ Stem cells → White blood cells (including lymphocytes) ]

Thus lymphocytes originate in **bone marrow of long bones**.

---

## C. Pancreas ❌ (Incorrect)

**Pancreas**

Functions:

• Produces insulin and glucagon
• Helps digestion

It has **no role in lymphocyte formation**.

---

## D. Spleen ❌ (Incorrect)

**Spleen**

The spleen:

• Stores and activates lymphocytes
• Filters blood

However, it **does not produce them initially**.

---

# Quick Summary

| Organ | Role |
|---|---|
| Bone marrow (long bones) | Produces lymphocytes |
| Spleen | Stores and activates lymphocytes |
| Liver | Metabolism and detoxification |
| Pancreas | Digestion and hormone secretion |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

**Blood cells are formed in bone marrow**.

So:

**Lymphocytes → Bone marrow → Long bones**.

questions.text: The cellular and molecular control of programmed cells death are known as
questions.solution:
This question is from **cell biology (cell death mechanisms)**, a topic that sometimes appears in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The cellular and molecular control of programmed cell death is known as**

A. Apoptosis
B. Ageing
C. Degeneration
D. Necrosis

✅ **Correct Answer: A. Apoptosis**

---

# Core Concept

**Apoptosis** is a **genetically regulated process** where cells die in a controlled manner.

It is often called **programmed cell death**.

Purpose:

• Removes damaged cells
• Maintains tissue balance
• Helps in normal development

Example:

• Removal of cells between fingers during embryo development.

---

# Features of Apoptosis

• Controlled by **genes and enzymes**
• Cell shrinks and breaks into small fragments
• Surrounding cells **phagocytose** the fragments
• **No inflammation occurs**

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Apoptosis ✔ (Correct)

Apoptosis is **regulated cell death** controlled by cellular and molecular mechanisms.

Functions:

• Tissue development
• Removal of harmful cells
• Prevention of cancer

Thus this is the correct answer.

---

## B. Ageing ❌ (Incorrect)

Ageing refers to **gradual decline in biological function over time**.

It is a **long-term process**, not a controlled death of individual cells.

---

## C. Degeneration ❌ (Incorrect)

Degeneration means **deterioration or damage of tissues or organs**.

It is usually associated with **disease or injury**, not programmed cell death.

---

## D. Necrosis ❌ (Incorrect)

**Necrosis** occurs due to:

• Infection
• Injury
• Lack of oxygen

Features:

• Sudden cell rupture
• Causes **inflammation**

It is **not programmed**.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Type of Cell Death | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| Apoptosis | Programmed and controlled |
| Necrosis | Uncontrolled due to damage |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

• **Apoptosis → programmed cell death**
• **Necrosis → accidental cell death**

Thus the correct answer is **Apoptosis**.
